currentUse: Original Lithuanian church building is gone; the parish survives as Holy Trinity–Epiphany Parish, worshipping in the basement of the 1963-built parish-hall building at 207 Adams St, Newark (Ironbound), now serving a Portuguese-speaking congregation with Mass no longer offered in Lithuanian.
summary: The "unnamed" Lithuanian parish in the archive record is identifiable as Holy Trinity (Švč. Trejybės) Lithuanian parish, founded in 1902 on Adams Street in Newark's Ironbound section. After the 1981 fire, the Archdiocese refused to permit repairs to the Romance Revival church, and the congregation relocated into the basement hall of the two-story parish hall it had built in 1963; the original church no longer stands. The parish itself was never suppressed: in 2002 it merged with neighboring Epiphany Parish to form Holy Trinity–Epiphany Parish, which the Archdiocese of Newark still lists as an active parish at 207 Adams St, Newark, NJ 07105. Mass is now Portuguese-only, the Portuguese-speaking (Ironbound) community having gradually displaced the Lithuanian one, though Lithuanian traces remain on site — a Vytis in the door glass, a Lithuanian folk-art frame around the Mass schedule, and a Lithuanian sun-cross near the entrance that dates to 1962 and was relocated from the old church. No press coverage of a property sale was found, consistent with the property remaining in diocesan/parish hands throughout. No recent preservation efforts, reunions, or Lithuanian community activity at the site were found in searches through July 2026.

developments.headline: Holy Trinity Lithuanian parish merged with Epiphany Parish
developments.detail: Per the Wikipedia list of churches in the Archdiocese of Newark, Holy Trinity Parish, 'founded in 1902 for Lithuanian immigrants, merged with Epiphany Parish in 2002,' forming the present Holy Trinity–Epiphany Parish at 207 Adams St. (A local church-history site, newarkreligion.com, gives the Lithuanian parish's span as 1902–2014, a possible alternate end-date for its distinct identity that could not be reconciled from available sources.)

sources.title: List of churches in the Archdiocese of Newark

developments.headline: Basement-hall church now Portuguese-only, with Lithuanian details surviving
developments.detail: Global True Lithuania documents that the congregation worships in the basement hall of the 1963 parish-hall building, that Mass is now Portuguese-only as the Portuguese-speaking community displaced the Lithuanian one, and that Lithuanian elements remain: a Vytis on the door glass, a Lithuanian folk-art wooden frame around the Mass schedule, and a Lithuanian sun-cross near the entrance standing since 1962, relocated from the old church.

sources.title: New Jersey: Lithuanians and Lithuanian heritage

developments.headline: Holy Trinity–Epiphany still listed as an active Archdiocese of Newark parish
developments.detail: The Archdiocese of Newark parish directory lists Holy Trinity–Epiphany Parish at 207 Adams Street, Newark, NJ 07105 as an active parish. GCatholic (page updated 2025-12-21) carries the same address and phone (973-344-6847); its language listing of 'Polish and Lithuanian' appears stale and contradicts on-the-ground reporting of Portuguese-only Mass. A pre-2026 Mass schedule on DiscoverMass shows English and Portuguese services with weekend and daily Masses, but carries a COVID-era caveat and no pastor name.

sources.title: Church of the Holy Trinity–Epiphany, Newark, New Jersey, USA

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: Three refinements to the archive record. (1) The parish is not unnamed: it is Holy Trinity (Švč. Trejybės) Lithuanian parish, founded 1902 on Adams Street, Newark — confirmed by newarkreligion.com, Wikipedia's Archdiocese of Newark list, and Global True Lithuania, whose account (1981 fire, diocese refused repairs, move to parish-hall basement, Portuguese use) matches the archive notes point for point. (2) 'Closed: 1981' and ending 'diocese_closed' overstate the case: 1981 is when the original church was lost, but the parish was not suppressed — it continued in the parish-hall basement, merged with Epiphany Parish in 2002 (Wikipedia; newarkreligion.com alternatively implies the Lithuanian identity lasted to 2014), and remains a canonically active parish (Holy Trinity–Epiphany) in the Archdiocese of Newark directory today. What ended in practice is the Lithuanian character of worship, not the parish. (3) The archive's 'demolished' applies to the original church building only; the 1963 parish-hall building stands and is in active parish use. Minor nuance: True Lithuania states the 1981 fire damage 'did not destroy' the church — demolition followed the diocese's refusal to permit repairs rather than the fire itself.
gaps: Demolition year of the original 1915-1922-era brick church not found — a Facebook post titled 'The demolition of Holy Trinity Lithuanian R.C. Church' exists but is login-walled and was not opened; no news article or record with a demolition date surfaced. No property-sale record searched for was found (none expected, as the property appears to have remained diocesan throughout). Current pastor could not be verified: one search snippet mentioned a Fr. George Joseph joining Holy Trinity from St. Paul of the Cross, Jersey City, but the underlying page was not opened and it may refer to a different Holy Trinity, so it is excluded from findings. Current 2026 Mass schedule unverified (DiscoverMass listing carries a COVID-era caveat; GCatholic's 'Polish and Lithuanian' language listing appears stale). The 2002 merger date (Wikipedia) vs. the 1902-2014 span on newarkreligion.com could not be reconciled — no diocesan announcement located. No recent press, preservation campaigns, reunions, or Lithuanian community activity found; Lithuanian-language searches (Švč. Trejybės Newark) returned encyclopedia entries (vle.lt returned HTTP 403) but no recent coverage. Archdiocese of Newark restructuring exposure for this parish not assessed — no specific announcement found.
